levels.body:
These lines of the poem constitute a metaphor: the speaker drinks the beams of the stars and experiences them as nurturing, which evokes the image of a mother breastfeeding her child.
Additionally, the syntax of the lines adds a level of ambiguity. The “as” can be read as (1) ’because’ or as (2) an ellipsis (as ‘if’). The first one reading creates a causal link between the two phrases and the second reading is a simile.

These lines refer to the speaker’s state of mind during the night-time, who experiences a feeling of peacefulness and serenity. The idea that the stars give nourishment to the speaker attributes them with a certain femininity. Janet Gezari compares the intimate relationship the speaker shares with the stars to the relationship between a mother and a child, by pointing to this “sensory exchange that evokes nursing” (30). The metaphor thus further describes the relationship between the speaker and the stars.
worksCited: Gezari, Janet. *Last Things: Emily Bronte’s Poems*. Oxford University Press, 2010.

levels.body: This intimate and sensory relationship between the speaker and the stars also becomes visible in the second stanza of the poem.
